1: The heads of the ancestral houses of the clan of Gilead come to Moses with a concern about the daughters of Zelophehad's inheritance. The community takes responsibility for the integrity of God's covenant arrangements — raising concerns about potential loopholes before they become problems. Christ calls his church to exercise the same vigilant stewardship of the covenant's integrity (Heb 12:15).
2: God commanded my lord to give the land by lot as an inheritance — and you were commanded to give the inheritance of our brother Zelophehad to his daughters. The appeal to divine command as the basis for their concern models covenant reasoning: the authority for the inheritance comes from God, therefore the integrity of the inheritance arrangement matters to God. Christ's people reason the same way — his commands are the basis for community standards (Matt 28:20).
3: If they are married to sons of any of the other tribes of Israel, their inheritance will be taken from our fathers' inheritance and added to the inheritance of the tribe into which they marry. The concern about cross-tribal marriage and inheritance loss reflects the OT's theology of territorial identity. In the new covenant, the inheritance cannot be lost to outside marriages because it is an imperishable spiritual inheritance, not a geographical one (1 Pet 1:4).
4: When the Jubilee comes, their land will be added permanently to the tribe into which they marry — thus it will be taken away from the inheritance of our ancestral tribe. The Jubilee complication — normally the restorer of tribal inheritance — becomes a threat if the daughters marry outside their tribe. The new covenant Jubilee is the resurrection, which permanently restores all inheritance without tribal complications: Christ's inheritance is secured beyond every loss mechanism (Luke 4:18–19; Isa 61:1–2).
5: Moses commands the Israelites at the word of the LORD: the tribe of the sons of Joseph is right. The validation of the tribal elders' concern — God upholds the community's interest in covenant integrity. Christ similarly validates his community's concern for the integrity of his covenant: 'Whatever you bind on earth shall be bound in heaven' (Matt 18:18).
6: This is what the LORD commands for the daughters of Zelophehad: they may marry whom they think best, but they shall marry within the clan of the tribe of their father. Marriage freedom within covenant constraint — the daughters may choose their own husbands, but within the tribe. This models the Christian's freedom within covenant bounds: 'she is free to be married to whom she wishes, only in the Lord' (1 Cor 7:39).
7: So no inheritance of the Israelites shall be transferred from one tribe to another, for every one of the Israelites shall hold on to the inheritance of the tribe of their ancestors. The permanence of tribal boundaries reflects the stability of God's covenant arrangements. The new covenant's permanent inheritance is even more secure: 'an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading' (1 Pet 1:4), which cannot be transferred, lost, or diluted.
8: Every daughter who possesses an inheritance in any tribe of the Israelites shall be wife to one of the clan of the tribe of her father, so that every Israelite may retain the inheritance of his ancestors. The universal principle derived from the Zelophehad case protects all daughters who inherit. The new covenant similarly protects all who have been given an inheritance in Christ — no believer's spiritual inheritance is at risk of being transferred to another or forfeited through earthly circumstances.
9: No inheritance shall be transferred from one tribe to another, for each of the tribes of the Israelites shall hold on to its own inheritance. The repetition of the no-transfer principle reinforces the covenant's structural integrity. Christ's promise of secured inheritance is equally emphatic and repeated: 'I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28).
10: The daughters of Zelophehad did as the LORD commanded Moses. Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Noah, the daughters of Zelophehad, married sons of their father's brothers. The five daughters obey — they marry within their own clan, accepting the constraint that preserves their inheritance. Obedience to God's ordering of the inheritance is not a burden but a protection; Christ's disciples discover that his yoke is easy precisely because it preserves rather than constricts true freedom (Matt 11:30).
11: They were married to sons of their father's brothers in the clans of the descendants of Manasseh son of Joseph, and their inheritance remained in the tribe of their father's clan. The inheritance preserved within the clan demonstrates that obedience to God's covenant ordering achieves exactly what it promises: the daughters retained their portion. Every act of covenant faithfulness preserves what would otherwise be lost; Christ's instruction preserves life, freedom, and inheritance for those who follow it (John 8:31–32).
12: These are the commandments and the rules that the LORD commanded through Moses to the people of Israel in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho. Numbers concludes with its summary formula — every command given, every ordinance established, at the threshold of the Promised Land. The entire Torah from Sinai to Moab points forward to the one greater than Moses who summarizes all commandments in two: love God and love neighbor (Matt 22:37–40; Deut 18:15).
13: The book of Numbers ends: these are the commandments and ordinances the LORD commanded through Moses to the Israelites in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho. The final words of Numbers situate the entire book at the Jordan threshold — the people of God poised to cross into the inheritance under Joshua. Christ is the true Joshua (Yēšūaʿ/Iēsous are the same name) who leads his people across the Jordan of death into the eternal inheritance: 'for if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on. So then, there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God' (Heb 4:8–9).
